Yes, it’s possible for the effects of cerebral palsy to develop later in life. While the disorder has traditionally been categorized as “developmental,” this designation is applied to a more severe case of the disorder that surfaces in infancy or early childhood, whereas adult onset of cerebral palsy can range from mild to severe, including motor and cognitive disabilities. This disorder has features such as multiple possible causes, varying presentation, and a complex interplay among genetic factors, brain development, and environmental influences. It is most accurate to describe CP as a complex and heterogeneous disorder that has no single classification scheme and is a product of many factors and changing conditions throughout a lifetime.

This is a good question, and definitely a very complicated issue.  Basically, in the United States and many other countries, the use of animals for testing cosmetics is not specifically regulated by law or regulated at all.  Rather, it is mostly regulated by the terms of the contracts between manufacturers and the testing laboratories.  So in general it is completely legal for a cosmetic manufacturer to contract with a testing laboratory to test their cosmetics on animals, and there’s really no regulatory oversight of that process or prohibition of that practice.  Now, the use of animals for testing cosmetics is widely viewed as ethically problematic, and organizations like PETA have lobbied hard to get manufacturers to stop using animal testing.  But at thi

s point I think most major cosmetics companies have agreed not to test their products on animals, so basically it’s just a few small, less well-known companies that still test cosmetics on animals.  I think it’s just kind of a lack of regulation of this process that makes it legal, but it’s definitely an issue that a lot of people are actively working to try and change.

For a long time scientists couldn’t explain why the outer core would be made of iron, or even if it was made of iron.  In 1960, oceanographers reported the observation that the core seems to consist of metallic iron.  Later, scientists used nuclear reactors and X-ray and magnetic resonance technology to more accurately determine its chemical composition.  Through these observations, it has been determined that the outer core has 60% iron, 20% nickel, 12% copper, 4% silicon and less than 1% sulfur.  

It has also been determined that the magnetic field is generated by currents in the liquid outer core caused by convection due to the high temperature there.  

It is also believed that the temperature in the outer core decreases at a rate of about a 1/4 °C per

thousand km (almost inversely proportional to the depth).  This temperature change causes convection currents, so the outer core has a dynamo effect similar to the one in the inner core.  

In the 1970s, satellite data showed that the pattern of the earth’s magnetic field does not match what scientists had predicted, based on the dynamo theory.  This discrepancy caused scientists to conclude that the ocean magnetic data they had been receiving must be flawed

People with frequent acid reflux should avoid eating or drinking a number of things that tend to promote acid reflux symptoms. These include: spicy foods, citrus fruits and juices, fried or fatty foods, greasy or soupy foods, tomato based foods, chocolate, alcohol, carbonated drinks, mint, onions, garlic, and caffeinated substances.  In addition, people with acid reflux symptoms should avoid foods that are known to be "reflux triggers," including: peppermint, coffee, fatty foods, fried or greasy foods, spicy foods, citrus fruits, chocolate, carbonated beverages, tomato-based foods, fried food, fatty food, milk, kefir, juice, alcohol, and high-carb beverages or foods. ife's challenges and making her own decisions, and that you should respect her choices even if you disagree with them. 

Another strategy that might be helpful is actually focusing your worry and concern on small actions you can take to actively help your daughter.  For example, maybe you could reach out to your daughter’s close friends and former teachers to get their input and recommendations regarding her situation.  You could also try engaging more directly with her in a spirit of supportive listening and advice-giving, rather than simply criticizing or correcting what she’s doing.  Overall it might be helpful to try to channel the strong emotions you are experiencing into more positive and productive actions aimed at helping your daughter.
